stories.title: Jury reaches split verdict in Shiloh Hendrix racial slur case
stories.summary: A jury in Minnesota has found Shiloh Hendrix guilty of a single misdemeanor count after she directed racial slurs at an 8-year-old girl with autism at a Rochester playground on April 28, 2025. She was acquitted of disorderly conduct but faces 200 hours of community service, supervised probation, and a $1,000 fine. The case has reignited debates over free speech and protected speech in public spaces.

stories.title: US-made F-16 helps Ukraine take out Russian Su-35 fighter jet for first time during war
stories.summary: A Ukrainian F-16 fighter jet has shot down a Russian Su-35 fighter jet for the first time during the war, marking a significant milestone in the country's air defenses. The US-made F-16, gifted to Ukraine in 2024, achieved the air-to-air kill in eastern Ukraine on July 8, according to General Dan Caine, the chairman of the US Joint Chiefs of Staff. This success highlights the effectiveness of the F-16s in bolstering Ukraine's air defenses against Russian attacks.

stories.title: ‘It’s generations of decline’: can Andy Burnham really end rough sleeping?
stories.summary: A makeshift shelter in Tooting, south London, shows rough sleeping has hit a record 4,793 people in England. Prime Minister Andy Burnham pledged £340m to end it, but charities warn rising numbers demand urgent action. Outreach teams report growing encampments with complex needs, from job losses to mental health crises.

stories.title: Indonesian babies were trafficked to Singapore. What happens to them now?
stories.summary: Indonesian babies trafficked to Singapore face an uncertain future after a court convicted 19 people for selling at least 34 infants, with 12 sent to Singapore. The verdict leaves adoptive parents in Singapore awaiting decisions on whether the children will stay or return to Indonesia. Authorities in both countries are now reviewing the cases to determine the next steps.

stories.title: Fired ‘60 Minutes’ Correspondent Says She Was Pushed to Report on Events ‘That Never Happened’ Under Bari Weiss’ Leadership at CBS News
stories.summary: Cecilia Vega, fired from CBS’s “60 Minutes” three months ago, says the new leadership under Bari Weiss pushed her to report unverified claims, including staged protests after the January 2026 killings of Renee Good and Alex Pretti in Minnesota. Vega told a journalism conference she and her team found no credible evidence to support the requested footage. CBS has not responded to requests for comment.

stories.title: AI helps Stanford scientists discover “natural Ozempic” without the usual side effects
stories.summary: Stanford Medicine researchers have discovered a natural molecule called BRP that mimics Ozempic’s appetite-suppressing effects without common side effects like nausea or muscle loss. In animal trials, BRP targeted only brain regions controlling hunger, unlike semaglutide, which affects multiple body systems. Human trials are planned to assess its potential as a safer weight-loss treatment.

stories.title: US hits dozens of countries with new wave of tariffs
stories.summary: The US has imposed new tariffs of 10% to 12.5% on goods from 60 countries, including the UK, China and the European Union, effective immediately. The move replaces an expiring 10% levy and targets imports accounting for nearly all American trade. Critics argue the tariffs aim to address forced labor concerns but may primarily serve broader trade policy goals.

stories.title: Chancellor joins PM on first day working from No 10 North
stories.summary: Chancellor Rachel Reeves joined Prime Minister Keir Starmer at No 10 North in Manchester on Friday, marking the first day of operations at the new government hub. Nine senior ministers, including Housing Secretary Angela Rayner and Business Secretary Jonathan Reynolds, are scheduled to hold meetings there to strengthen regional policy coordination. The move signals a long-term commitment to decentralising power from London across all regions and nations.

stories.title: Music’s Legal Elite 2026: Meet the Attorneys Who Help the Stars Shine
stories.summary: Variety’s 2026 Music’s Legal Elite list names top attorneys shaping the industry, including John Branca, Danielle Aguirre and Jeff Harleston. These lawyers negotiate billion-dollar deals and protect artists’ rights amid AI threats and contract pitfalls. The rankings reflect their 2025-26 achievements in securing fair terms for stars and catalogs.

stories.title: Tens of thousands evacuated from French tourist spot and near Madrid as wildfires spread
stories.summary: Tens of thousands of people are being evacuated from the Cap Ferret peninsula in southwest France and areas near Madrid as wildfires rage across both countries. French officials report 10,000 hectares burned in the Gironde region, while Spain declares a national emergency with blazes described as the worst in Madrid’s history. Firefighters are battling the flames amid worsening conditions.

stories.title: ‘Tomorrow, and Tomorrow, and Tomorrow’ Adds ‘Last Days’ Actor Sky Yang, ‘Summer I Turned Pretty’ Star Sean Kaufman (EXCLUSIVE)
stories.summary: Paramount’s adaptation of Gabrielle Zevin’s best-selling novel “Tomorrow, and Tomorrow, and Tomorrow” will star Sky Yang and Sean Kaufman alongside Daisy Edgar-Jones. The film follows childhood friends Sadie and Sam Masur over decades as they reunite in college and collaborate on video games. Production is set to begin soon.

stories.title: Whack-a-drone
stories.summary: The US Federal Communications Commission banned foreign-made drones on December 22, 2025, citing national security risks. Investigators found Odyssey Robot’s Pasadena office in California empty, with no equipment or staff, despite claiming to manufacture drones. The ban now faces scrutiny as critics call it ineffective.

stories.title: Tanzanian lecturer arrested over anti-government protests charged with terrorism
stories.summary: A Tanzanian university lecturer, Melkisedek Kaijage, has been charged with terrorism over his alleged role in inciting anti-government protests. Kaijage, who teaches political science and public administration at the University of Dodoma, was arrested on July 6 and initially charged with inciting unrest, but the charges were upgraded after 15 days in police custody. He will remain in custody until his case goes to trial, as terrorism offences are generally not eligible for bail.

stories.title: England captain Sciver-Brunt to miss first two Hundred matches
stories.summary: England captain Nat Sciver-Brunt will miss the first two matches of The Hundred due to a calf injury she has been managing since April, when she suffered the problem playing domestic cricket. The 33-year-old all-rounder had to play as a specialist batter in the T20 World Cup after missing several matches, including England's final loss to Australia at Lord's. Sciver-Brunt's absence will be felt as Trent Rockets begin their Hundred campaign on Friday against Birmingham Phoenix.

stories.title: Why Jimothy the raccoon has become a mega meme online
stories.summary: A Seattle raccoon named Jimothy has gone viral after a video showed him scurrying up steps, sparking jokes and memes about his short spine syndrome. The clip, posted by Kiana Hall on Instagram, has racked up over eight million views and inspired calls to care for the animal. Experts note Jimothy joins a growing trend of creatures gaining fame for conditions that highlight animal welfare issues.

stories.title: Tracee Ellis Ross Is Being Praised For Her No BS Response To “Bizarre” People Asking Why She Doesn’t Have Kids
stories.summary: Actress Tracee Ellis Ross has been praised for her blunt response to people asking why she doesn't have children, saying "Will you get out of my womb?" and "Stop!" in a recent interview. The comment has resonated with child-free individuals who often face invasive and ignorant questions about their life choices. Ross's unapologetic response is being hailed as a refreshing example of assertiveness in the face of unwanted curiosity.

stories.title: Iran War and Trump’s Tariffs Threaten a Resilient U.S. Economy
stories.summary: As renewed fighting with Iran sends oil and gas prices soaring, President Trump has imposed new global tariffs that could push up prices further.

stories.title: Clock Ticks Down to Europe’s Ban on Russian Gas Tankers
stories.summary: Europeans say they fear a rising dependence on American energy even with a looming Jan. 1 deadline to end liquefied natural gas imports from Russia.

stories.title: Russia and Ukraine Trade Deadly Blows With Missiles and Drones
stories.summary: Ukrainian strikes set fire to warehouses and other sites in Russia, and Russian bombing rocked Ukraine’s capital, as the two countries escalated an air campaign.

stories.title: Brent Crude Oil Prices Top $100 as Conflict With Iran Drags On
stories.summary: Stocks in Asia were down following a sell-off in the U.S. markets over soaring oil prices, rising borrowing costs and worries about the technology industry.

stories.title: South Africa's president wins bid to temporarily halt impeachment inquiry over 'Farmgate'
stories.summary: South Africa's President Cyril Ramaphosa has won a temporary reprieve from an impeachment inquiry over the "Farmgate" scandal, with a court ruling in his favour. The inquiry stems from a 2022 incident in which burglars stole large sums of cash hidden in a sofa from Ramaphosa's private farm, which he denied any wrongdoing in. The president's challenge to the findings of an independent panel will now be heard in a separate court case in September.

stories.title: FDA Panel Supports Lifting Restrictions on Four Peptides
stories.summary: In an all-day hearing, the agency’s scientists argued against allowing broad access to unproven drugs that the agency had banned three years ago.

stories.title: Iran Rejects U.S. Cease-Fire Deal Presented by Iraqi Leader
stories.summary: The details of the proposal were not immediately clear, but Iranian officials said that it was the only offer on the table.

stories.title: TikTok’s protection of minors should not be opt-in, warns EU
stories.summary: The European Commission has issued preliminary findings under the Digital Services Act, warning TikTok that its default settings for minors’ public accounts do not adequately protect children. The EU demands changes so content from teens is only visible to approved followers and not recommended in the For You feed. TikTok could face fines of up to 6 percent of its global annual revenue if it fails to comply.

stories.title: Hubble Spies One-Sided Spiral
stories.summary: NASA’s Hubble Space Telescope has captured NGC 4654, a spiral galaxy 72 million light-years away in the Virgo Cluster, showing one side stripped of gas by ram pressure. The galaxy’s lopsided shape reveals a long gaseous tail on one edge as it plows through the cluster’s thin, hot gas. Astronomers say this stripping process strips away star-forming material, altering the galaxy’s evolution.

stories.title: One year, 17 towns, dozens of voices: what we heard along the coast of England and Wales
stories.summary: A devastating trend has emerged along the coast of England and Wales, where a 73% decline in funding for youth services over the past 12 years has left nearly 1 million 16- to 24-year-olds without work, education, or training, with many young people feeling isolated and despondent in their communities. This stark reality has been exacerbated by negative media portrayals of young people in these areas, often described as "lazy" or "feral". The Against the Tide project will continue to report on the lives of young people in these coastal communities over the next year.

stories.title: I spent years trying to get smaller. Until I decided to get bigger
stories.summary: A woman who had spent years lifting weights to stay slim suddenly faced body-image struggles after her boyfriend proposed in a museum. Planning the wedding triggered a strict diet and exercise routine that spiraled into unhealthy eating patterns. She now warns others about the dangers of extreme weight loss tactics ahead of big life events.

stories.title: Replacing gas stove can help asthmatics as much as some drugs, US experts find
stories.summary: A US study found replacing gas stoves with electric cut hospital visits and missed school or work by 70% for people with poorly controlled asthma. The project, involving 1,200 Cleveland-area residents, was cut short in December 2024 after funding ended early. Even with just 72 homes analyzed, the improvements matched or exceeded results from common asthma medications.
